content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 14,
1996
Norman Y.
Herring
City Attorney
City of Culver City
9770 Culver Boulevard
Culver City,
California
90232—0507
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-96-162
Dear Mr.
Herring:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
City of Culver City Counciliuember Sandra Levin regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) •1
QUESTION
May Councilmember Levin participate in decisions affecting
the following:
1)
Her fiance’s city clerk salary from Culver City;
2)
The budget of city clerk’s office,
apart from her
fiance’s salary;
3)
Policy matters which may affect the city clerk’s office.
CONCLUSION
1)
Councilmember Levin may participate in decisions
affecting her fiance’s salary only if the decision does not
increase or decrease her assets or expenses by $250 or more.
Once
Councilmember Levin is married,
she may participate in decisions
affecting her spouse’s salary,
expenses and per diem received from
the city,
but she may not participate in decisions to hire, fire,
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
Commission regulations
appear at Title 2,
Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—162
Page
2
promote or set her spouse’s salary if it is different from
salaries paid to other employees in the same job classification.
2)
Councilmember Levin may participate in decisions
affecting the budget of the clerk’s office only if the decisions
do not increase or decrease her own assets or expenses by $250 or
more.
This rule applies both before and after she is married.
3)
Councilmember Levin may participate in policy matters
affecting the city clerk’s office only if the decisions do not
increase or decrease her own assets or expenses by $250 or more.
This rule also applies both before or after she is married.
FACTS
Councilmember Levin and the city clerk of Culver City,
an
elected official who does not report to the city council, are
engaged to be married in September.
At the present time,
Councilmember Levin and the city clerk own real property together
in Culver City and share certain household expenses.
The city
clerk has an income other than his city salary that constitutes a
source of income in excess of $250 per year.
Generally, city councilinembers review the budget of the city
clerk’s office on an annual basis; they also review and decide
policy questions concerning operations of the city which affect
the city clerk’s department.
The city clerk’s salary is set by
ordinance.
ANALYSIS
The Act provides a variety of obligations on public officials
in connection with the receipt of gifts and income.
First,
every public official must disclose all his or her
economic interests that could fbreseeably be affected by the
exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87200—
87313.)
A “public official”- is defined broadly to include every
natural person who is a member, officer,
employee,
or consultant
of a state or local government agency.
(Section 82048; Regulation
18700.)
Section 87207 provides that a public official must
disclose the name and address of each source of gifts of $50 or
more in value, the amount and the date on which the gift was
received,
and that the official must provide a general description
of the business activity of the donor.
Further, the official must
disclose each source of income aggregating $250 or more.
(Section
87207(a) .)
Second,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any
level of state or local government from making, participating in
making or in any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know the official has a financial
interest.
(Section 87100.)
An official has a financial interest


File No.
A—96—162
Page
3
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on any source of income aggregating $250 or more or on
any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for a donor of,
a gift
or gifts aggregating $280 or more in value provided to, received
by,
or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to
the time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(c)
&
(e).)
Third, the Act now has a gift limit and honorarium ban for
local officials.
(Section 89503.)
Section 89503(a)
provides that
no elected officer of a local government agency shall accept gifts
from any single source in any calendar year with a total value of
more than $280.
Section 89502 provides that no official shall
accept an honorarium.
Gifts
The definition of gift,
however, specifically excludes gifts
from an official’s spouse.
(Section 82028(b)(3).)
Therefore,
once Ms.
Levin and her fiance are married,
she will not have to
disclose gifts received from her spouse.
For gifts received prior to the marriage,
the Commission has
carved a narrow exception from the definition of “gift” for gifts
exchanged within the context of a “bona fide dating relationship.”
In such a relationship, truly personal gifts such as gifts of
entertainment, meals,
personal property,
or expenses involved in
recreational travel are not prohibited and need not be disclosed
on an official’s Statement of Economic Interests.
These types of
gifts do not create a conflict of interest and are not disqualify
ing.
The Commission has held that these relationships are similar
to family or spousal relationships in which personal gifts are
frequently exchanged and disclosure of such gifts would not
further the purposes of the Act.
(Shea Advice Letter,
No.
A—84—085.)
Thus,
any payments madeby the city clerk for gifts and his
share of household expenses would not be considered gifts for
purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest disclosure and
disqualification provisions,
as well as the gift limits of the
Act.
2
(Larson Advice Letter,
No.
A—96-l55.)
2
Please note that “gifts which are not truly personal in nature
but are primarily related to business activities
(such as money to
be used for the official’s business or to purchase business
property)
must be disclosed on the official’s statement of
economic interest even if they are from a person with whom the
official has a bona fide dating relationship.”
(Roff Advice
Letter,
No.
A-89—l06.)


File No.
A—96—162
Page
4
Income
Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received
from a state, local or federal government agency is expressly
exempted from the definition of “income” for purposes of the Act.
(Section 82030(b)(2).)
Consequently, the salary the city clerk
receives from the city is not considered income to the city clerk
under the Act.
The city clerk’s office will not be a source of
income to either Councilmember Levin or her fiance.
As a result,
Councilmember Levin may participate in policy and budget decisions
affecting the Office of the City Clerk both before and after her
marriage.
The city clerk as an individual could be a source of income
to Councilmember Levin prior to the marriage.
However,
we do not
have any facts indicating that is the case.
As noted above, gifts
received from the city clerk will be subject to the bona fide
dating exception and we have no facts indicating that
Councilmember Levin receives any other income from the city clerk.
The Act,
however,
also provides that a conflict of interest
may exist where a decision will result in a public official’s
personal expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities or those of his
or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by $250.
(Regulation l8702.l(a)(4).)
Therefore, if Councilmember Levin
makes a decision regarding her fiance’s salary or employment that
will increase or decrease her personal expenses by $250,
then she
must disqualify herself from the decision.
This would also be the
case once they are married.
Section 82030(a), which defines “income,” provides that the
income of an individual includes any community property interest
in the income of a spouse.
However,
an official does not have to
disqualify from participating in a governmental decision if the
decision only affects the salary, per diem or reimbursement for
expenses the official or his or her spouse receives from a state
or local government agency.
(Regulation 18702.1(c) (1).)
As a
result,
once Councilmember Lévin is married, if a decision comes
before the district that will affect the salary of the city clerk,
Councilmember Levin would not be required to disqualify herself
from the decision.
2
2
However, please note that the exception in Regulation
18702.1(c) (1)
does not apply to decisions to hire, fire,
promote,
demote or discipline an official’s spouse,
or to set a salary for
an official’s spouse which is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the spouse’s agency in the same job
classification or position.
(Regulation 18702.l(c)(1).)
We do
not have enough facts to indicate whether the exception would
apply in this case.
If the city clerk’s salary is set in
conjunction with several other high level city officials, then it
may apply.
If,
however,
the city clerk’s salary is considered
independently, the exception probably would not apply.


File No.
A—96--162
Page
5
You also stated that the city clerk receives income other
than his city income.
Pursuant to Section 82030(a),
Councilmeinber
Levin will have an economic interest in her community property
interest in that income.
Accordingly, after the marriage,
she
must disqualify herself from any decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the source of
the city clerk’s nongovernmental income.
3
If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Liane Randolph
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak
You also mentioned that Councilmember Levin owns real property
with her fiance.
Please keep in mind that an official may not
participate in a decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the official’s real property
interest.
(Section87103(b).)
If you have any issue that arise
with regard to the real property interest, please feel free to
request further advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Levin participate in decisions affecting
the following:
1)
Her fiance’s city clerk salary from Culver City;
2)
The budget of city clerk’s office,
apart from her
fiance’s salary;
3)
Policy matters which may affect the city clerk’s office.

CONCLUSION: 1)
Councilmember Levin may participate in decisions
affecting her fiance’s salary only if the decision does not
increase or decrease her assets or expenses by $250 or more.
Once
Councilmember Levin is married,
she may participate in decisions
affecting her spouse’s salary,
expenses and per diem received from
the city,
but she may not participate in decisions to hire, fire,

(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—162
promote or set her spouse’s salary if it is different from
salaries paid to other employees in the same job classification.
2)
Councilmember Levin may participate in decisions
affecting the budget of the clerk’s office only if the decisions
do not increase or decrease her own assets or expenses by $250 or
more.
This rule applies both before and after she is married.
3)
Councilmember Levin may participate in policy matters
affecting the city clerk’s office only if the decisions do not
increase or decrease her own assets or expenses by $250 or more.
This rule also applies both before or after she is married.
